coredata.dc:description: Metal-organic framework (MOF)-derived carbon-based electrodes have demonstrated outstanding capacitive deionization (CDI) performance, owing to their special structures and configurations. Developing novel low-cost controllable protocols to obtain highly efficient Nitrogen-doped carbon-derived MOFs is the most challenging factor to transfer widely the application of these materials at real world. This work aims to develop a low-cost one-step growth route to produce N-doped carbon (NGZC) utilizing N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and zeolitic imidazolate frameworks-8 (ZIF-8) as starting substrates. The surface chemistry, morphology and CDI performance of the as-synthesized NGZC electrode were investigated, compared with conventional materials. The authors found that the introduction of N-doped graphene quantum dots dramatically enhances the mesopore volume (0.742 cm3/g) and pyrrolic-N ratio (19.21 %) for NGZC. These improvements effectively shorten the electron and sodium ion transport paths which in turn facilitate the pseudocapacitive activity and charge transfer in NGZC. The specific capacitance and salt adsorption capacity were found to be 283.16 F/g and 17.86 mg/g, respectively, demonstrating superior values than commonly used electrodes. NGZC shows a high stability over 30 adsorption-desorption cycles with a capacity retention of 90 %. DFT results further demonstrate the pseudocapacitive superiority of pyrrolic-N to reinforce the Na+ capture. This finding provides new insights into the design of an economical and controllable N-doped carbon electrode for efficient CDI desalination.

head.ce:title: One-step controllable synthesis of N-doped porous carbon derived from ZIF-8 for efficient capacitive deionization

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Writing- original draft, Software, Data curation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-organic framework (MOF)-derived carbon-based electrodes have demonstrated outstanding capacitive deionization (CDI) performance, owing to their special structures and configurations. Developing novel low-cost controllable protocols to obtain highly efficient Nitrogen-doped carbon-derived MOFs is the most challenging factor to transfer widely the application of these materials at real world. This work aims to develop a low-cost one-step growth route to produce N-doped carbon (NGZC) utilizing N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and zeolitic imidazolate frameworks-8 (ZIF-8) as starting substrates. The surface chemistry, morphology and CDI performance of the as-synthesized NGZC electrode were investigated, compared with conventional materials. The authors found that the introduction of N-doped graphene quantum dots dramatically enhances the mesopore volume (0.742 cm3/g) and pyrrolic-N ratio (19.21 %) for NGZC. These improvements effectively shorten the electron and sodium ion transport paths which in turn facilitate the pseudocapacitive activity and charge transfer in NGZC. The specific capacitance and salt adsorption capacity were found to be 283.16 F/g and 17.86 mg/g, respectively, demonstrating superior values than commonly used electrodes. NGZC shows a high stability over 30 adsorption-desorption cycles with a capacity retention of 90 %. DFT results further demonstrate the pseudocapacitive superiority of pyrrolic-N to reinforce the Na+ capture. This finding provides new insights into the design of an economical and controllable N-doped carbon electrode for efficient CDI desalination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid growth of industrialization and population worldwide has led to a growing scarcity of freshwater resources, which presents a significant challenge to global sustainable development[1]. Water desalination is one of the most widely used sustainable technologies to address global freshwater scarcity, and to limit the overconsumption of groundwater. A number of techniques are commercially available and/or under development, ranging from thermal distillation, ion exchange, reverse osmosis, electrodialysis, solar photothermal desalination, etc[2,3]. Each of these techniques exhibits pros and cons towards seawater desalination. In general, there is a great demand for novel techniques and/or enhanced processes that combine high efficiency, simplicity, low-cost and sustainability to be alternatives to currently used routes, especially with the recent worldwide sustainable development movement. Among these techniques, capacitive deionization (CDI) is sustainable and effective easy-to-handle technique at low operating cost and low energy requirements[4]. Thanks to these characteristics, CDI has captured increasing attention as a promising alternative seawater desalination technology. CDI is based on the enhanced removal of ions via electric double-layer (EDL) generated at the solution-electrode interface[5]. In particular, under an applied electrostatic field, ions are removed by attraction, resulting in their deposition on the electrode surface. Once the electrostatic field is removed, the accumulated ions are automatically released into the concentrate, regenerating the electrode for further use[6]. The performance of this technique relies fundamentally on the characteristics of the used electrodes and their capacity to host as much as possible ions from seawater. In this sense, both the electrochemical properties and the surface porosity and functionality are crucial factors that need to be taken into consideration during the synthesis process of CDI-based electrodes.

Carbon-based electrodes are of great interest for CDI processes on account of their high specific surface area, good porous structures, and favorable electrical conductivity. This includes activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, mesoporous carbon, and carbon aerogels etc[7]. However, these conventional carbon materials possess a limited number of adsorption sites and an inferior energy density, which results in a low CDI desalination capacity and rate[8]. In order to address this challenge, metal-organic framework (MOFs)-derived carbon has been developed in recent years, facilitating with regular pore structures and excellent capacitive properties[9]. Specifically, Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8), a subspecies of MOFs, stands out as a promising precursor to synthesize hierarchical porous carbon for high-performance of CDI desalination. Owing to the fact that ZIF-8 is composed of a nitrogen-containing heterocyclic compound, ZIF-8 is used frequently to optimize the pore structure and improve hydrophilicity[10]. For example, Wang et al.[11] obtained nano-porous carbon material using ZIF-8 as the precursor for enhanced CDI performance, through tannic acid etching and high-temperature carbonization. However, it is worth noting that the MOF-derived carbon materials are mostly dominated by micropores, which inherently restricts salt ion diffusion and access to the internal space, thus impairing the CDI performance[12]. For these reasons, it is fundamental to modify the ZIF-8 surface for improving the quantity of mesopores, which serve as intermediate channels for ions to enter the micropores. This facilitates the formation of the electric double layer (EDL) on the inner surface, thereby ensuring good circulation performance and a high salt removal rate[13].

N-doping, has been extensively employed to modify carbon materials, can effectively induce a multitude of mesoporous structures and defects on ZIF-8 surface[14]. Meanwhile, N-doping can enhance the charge transport efficiency and hydrophobicity of carbon materials, thereby achieving a high CDI desalination capacity[15]. Tian et al.[16] reported a N-doped highly-ordered mesoporous carbon fabricated by a self-assembly method, using 1,6-hexamethylenediamine (C6H16N2) as nitrogen source that can attain a remarkable desalination capacity. Notably, the surfactant-mediated approach is commonly used for N-doped ZIF-8, which facilitates the stabilization of nanocrystal growth during the nucleation stage and the regulation of the mesopore-to-micropore ratio distribution[17]. Zheng et al. [18] synthesized a variety of ZIF-8 shapes and porous structures by controlling the number of surfactants. In addition, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a zero-dimensional semiconductor material, are emerging as a promising candidate for an N-doped electrode in efficient CDI desalination, due to their strong quantum confinement and edge effects[19]. The N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have the potential to enhance pseudocapacitive activity and facilitate charge transfer between N-GQDs and electrolytes. Nevertheless, two significant obstacles to the advancement of N-doped ZIF-8 and N-doped GQD, namely the precise control of hierarchical porosity and the appropriate regulation of pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N, have hindered their further development. Furthermore, the synthesis protocols for N-doped electrodes are complex, involving multiple steps such as the introduction of diverse templates and the removal of these templates, as well as the use of costly raw materials [20]. Therefore, it is of great interest to develop a simple synthetic strategy that can achieve precise control of hierarchical porosity and appropriate regulation of pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N simultaneously, with the aim of fabricating a highly functional ZIF-8 derived CDI electrode.

Herein, we successfully synthesized a novel N-doped carbon (NGZC) via a low-cost one-step in-situ growth process using N-GQDs and ZIF-8. This eliminates the need for multi-step reactions involving diverse templates and costly raw materials. Moreover, this innovative synthesis method combines the precise control of hierarchical porosity and appropriate regulation of pyrrolic-N, thereby enhancing the CDI performance of NGZC. To support this, the surface chemistry, morphology and electrochemical adsorption capacity of the as-synthesized NGZC electrode were investigated, in comparison with conventional materials. Thanks to the multiple advantages, in which 1) the well-developed porosity effectively shortens the diffusion path for electron and sodium ion transport, 2) the high degree of graphitization and the presence of significant defects of NGZC improves its electrical conductivity, and 3) the incorporation of more favorable nitrogen dopants (pyrrolic-N) significantly enhances the pseudocapacitive activity and charge transfer, NGZC exhibits a huge specific capacitance of 283.16 F/g and a good salt adsorption capacity (SAC) of 17.86 mg/g. This represents a significant improvement in performance compared to previously reported N-doped ZIF-derived porous carbon electrodes. Finally, we employed the DFT calculations to better understand the role of pyrrolic-N in enhancing the pseudocapacitance and SACs in CDI desalination process. This work contributes to a deeper understanding of the design principles for developing high-performance electrode materials based on N-doped ZIF-derived porous carbon for CDI applications.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate, 2-methylimidazole (2-MeIM), sodium chloride (NaCl), N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), tannic acid (TA) and carbon black were supplied by Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co. Ltd. Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), citric acid (CA), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and urea were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Deionized water (DI water) with a resistance greater than 18.2 MΩ was used throughout the experiments. All reagents were analytically pure and were used without further purification.

The synthesis protocol of NGZC is shown in Fig. 1a. First, we mixed 0.75 g of zinc nitrate hexahydrate, 0.5 g of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), and 10 mL of N-GQDs solution in 100 mL of deionized water at room temperature, designated as solution A. Second, we dissolved 10.85 g of 2-methylimidazole in 100 mL of deionized water, and introduced 4 mL of CTAB (0.01 M) drop by drop into the mixture to obtain solution B. Third, solution B was added to solution A, after 24 hours of stirring, the mixture was centrifuged and washed thrice with methanol to selectively remove CTAB, and then dried in a vacuum oven at 75 ℃ for 24 h to obtain the N-GQDs@ZIF-8 composite material (NGZ). Finally, the NGZ was pyrolyzed at 1000 ℃ for 3 h under an N2 atmosphere, after cooling, the carbonized NGZ was immersed in a 2 M HCl solution and stirred for 24 h. This step could ensure the complete decomposition and removal of any remaining surfactant residues, as CTAB decomposes into volatile byproducts at temperatures above 400°C[21]. Then the NGZC was obtained by rinsing and drying at 80 ℃ overnight. The ZIF-8 crystal (ZC) and N-GQDs were synthesized via the methods employed by Shen and Ganganboina et al.[11,22].

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SU8020) and field emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30) were employed to analyze the surface morphology and nanoscale of NGZC composites. The ASAP 2000 (Micromeritics) was used to determine the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) specific surface area and pore volume distribution. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Brucker D8 advance) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) were applied to investigate the phase composition, elemental contents and states. Raman spectroscopy (Raman, Renishaw India) was used to characterize the crystal structure of the prepared samples.

The electrochemical tests were carried out in a three-electrode system. A glass carbon, saturated Ag/AgCl and as prepared NGZC composites were adopted as the counter, reference, and working electrodes, respectively. The working electrode area used in the electrochemical tests was 1 cm × 1 cm. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy(EIS) were performed in the 1 mol/L NaCl solution at room temperature using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instruments Co., China). The scan rates of the CV measurements were 5,10,20,50 and 100 mV s−1, the specific currents used in the GCD tests were 0.5,1,2,3,4 and 5 A g−1, and the applied voltage ranged from −1 V to 1 V. The EIS measurements were conducted over a frequency range of 0.1 Hz to 100000 Hz. The specific capacitances (Cs, F/g) obtained from CV curves and GCD tests were calculated by the Eq. S1 and Eq. S2 presented in Text SI.

The performance of electrodes for CDI was evaluated by a continuous circulatory system (Fig. 1b), including a batch-mode CDI cell with a pair of parallel PC electrodes, a DC stabilized power supply (DF-1731SB3A, Shanghai PZ Bio-Tech Co., Ltd), a peristaltic pump, and a conductivity meter (S230, Mettler Toledo). The CDI cell was constructed with various NGZC electrodes, prepared by mixing active materials, carbon black, PVDF with a mass ratio of 8:1:1 in NMP, coating the slurry on graphite plates, and drying in 80 ℃ for 12 h.

Particularly, in CDI experiments, 50 mL NaCl solution with different salt concentrations (100, 250 and 500 mg/L) is continuously pumped into the CDI system with a constant flow rate of 20 mL/min. The device voltages are set at 1.0, 1.2 and 1.4 V, respectively. The real-time conductivity of the NaCl solution was monitored by a conductivity meter during the charging/discharging process of the CDI system. The correlation between NaCl concentration and electrical conductivity was shown in Fig S1. The Salt Adsorption Capacity (SAC) (mg/g) and Salt Adsorption Rate (SAR) (mg /g∙ min) were calculated by the Eq. S3 and Eq. S4 presented in Text SI. All the experiments were repeated three times.

The DFT was employed using the Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP) version 6.3.2 to perform structural optimization and energy calculation[23]. The adsorption energies between different N species (graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N), pristine graphene and Na⁺ were calculated for comparison. The projected augmented wave (PAW) potentials were chosen to describe the ionic cores and take valence electrons into account using a plane wave basis set with a kinetic energy cutoff of 400 eV. All relaxations were done until the force of the system converges to 0.02 eV/Å and the energy converges to the 10−5 eV atom−1. To minimize the interlayer interactions, the vacuum distance was larger than 20 Å.

The unit conversion formula for adsorption energy is as follows:
(1) 1 eV = 96.49 kJ/mol

The adsorption energy of Na (E ads ) was calculated according to the following equation:
(2) E ads = E X-Na - E X - E Na
where E ads is the adsorption energy between adsorbents (including pristine graphene, graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N). E X-Na refers to the optimal adsorption configuration of the adsorbents and Na⁺. E X and E Na are the total energies of the adsorbents and Na⁺ in the structure.

The morphology and microstructure of N-GQDs, NGZ and NGZC were characterized by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. S2, the modified ZIF-8 showed a cubic structure when the surfactant was modified with CTAB. However, NGZ and NGZC particles transformed from cubic to rhombic dodecahedral structure (Fig. S3). This is attributed to the addition of PVP during the encapsulation of N-GQDs into ZIF-8 crystals, which is commonly used as a surfactant or capping agent to stabilize or regulate the size and shape of the nanoparticles[24].

Specifically, Fig. 2a shows the homogeneously distributed N-GQDs with uniform particle sizes. For single N-GQDs lattice, the fringe pattern is clearly observed and the spacing is calculated to be 0.21 nm (Fig. 2b), which corresponds to the (100) plane of single crystal graphene. This indicates the successful formation of sp2 hybridized carbon nanostructures[25]. After encapsulating the N-GQDs inside the ZIF-8 using a one-step in situ growth method, the distribution of N-GQDs inside the NGZ can be seen clearly (Fig. 2c). The fringe distance of 0.21 nm corresponds to the characteristic lattice plane of N-GQDs (Fig. 2d), confirming the successful encapsulation of N-GQDs within ZIF-8.

Following the carbonization process, NGZC surface remains the presence of N-GQDs with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, but with particle sizes smaller than 5 nm (Figs. 2e, 2 f). This is because the size of the N-GQDs changed towards the dimensional properties of the GO nanosheets as the duration and temperature of the carbonization process increased [26]. Moreover, the boundary of the NGZC hexahedron cross-section becomes less distinct, and the shadow color of the N-GQDs inside the NGZC becomes lighter (Fig. 2e). This suggests that the quantity of N-GQDs present in the carbonized NGZC is less than that of N-GQDs in the NGZ prior to carbonization.

XRD analysis demonstrates that the characteristic peaks of NGZ align perfectly with those of the pristine ZIF-8 ( Fig. 3a). The diffraction peaks at 2θ = 7.32°, 10.37°, 12.71°, 14.69°, 16.43°, and 18.02° are attributed to the planes of (011), (002), (112), (022), (013), and (222), respectively, for standard ZIF-8 [27]. The findings suggest that the NGZ retains the crystalline properties of ZIF-8. After carbonization, NGZC exhibits two broad diffraction peaks of (002) and (101) at 25°and 44°, respectively (Fig. 3b), indicating a high degree of carbonization for NGZC[28]. This result is in accordance with that of ZC, which represents the carbonized ZIF-8. However, the (002) diffraction peak intensity of NGZC is greater than that of ZC, indicating a higher degree of graphitization in NGZC.

Raman spectra confirm this graphitized structure and further investigate surface defects and amorphous degrees of NGZC and ZC (Fig. 3c). Two prominent peaks were observed at 1342 and 1579 cm−1, which have been previously assigned to the D band for the defective graphitic structure, and the G band corresponds to graphitic layers[29]. The intensity ratio of I
D
/I
G is approximately 1.02 for NGZC, signifying a higher proportion of graphitic carbon than that of ZC (0.99). This indicates that the incorporation of N-GQDs has the effect of increasing the degree of graphitization and the prevalence of defects in NGZC, which is in accordance with the XRD analysis presented in Fig. 3b. The enhanced graphitization and defects demonstrate the presence of plentiful functionalized surface active sites, which are conducive to the accumulation of charges and the absorption of ions during the desalination process[30].

N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were adopted to analysis the specific surface area (SSA) and pore structure of NGZC and ZC. Fig. 3d illustrates that NGZC exhibits a combination of characteristics associated with both microporous and mesoporous materials, as evidenced by the shape of nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm. Particularly, at low pressure (p/p0 < 0.1), a considerable quantity of nitrogen is adsorbed, and the adsorption curve rises rapidly, indicating that the NGZC contains a multitude of microporous structures. These micropores provide numerous active sites for ion adsorption during desalination, thereby enhancing the desalination process[31]. As the relative pressure increases (0.2 < p/p0 < 0.8), the isotherm shows a distinct H4 hysteresis loop, which is characteristic of cylindrical or ink-bottle shaped mesopores. This feature confirms that the surfactant-mediated synthesis approach successfully introduces mesoporous channels into NGZC, facilitating faster ion transport [32]. Ultimately, at higher pressures (p/p0 > 0.8), the upward shift in the adsorption curve further indicates the presence of macropores within the material. These macropores act as ion-buffering reservoirs, reducing the diffusion distance to the inner surface and improving overall ion accessibility [33]. Collectively, the coexistence of micropores, mesopores, and macropores in NGZC forms an interconnected three-dimensional network structure. This hierarchical pore architecture not only enhances ion transport but also improves the conductivity of NGZC, making it highly effective for desalination applications [34].

Most importantly, the pore size distribution of ZC and NGZC provides further evidence to support the above findings, as illustrated in Fig. 3e. Notably, the dominant pore size for NGZC is mesopore, which is predominantly situated within the range of 2.0–5.0 nm. This can facilitate an enhanced rate of ion transport during CDI desalination. In contrast, the pore size of ZC is predominantly distributed within the range of 1.0–2.0 nm, which corresponds to micropores. This suggests that ion transport is limited due to the absence of mesopores, which would otherwise provide a low-resistance channel[35].

Correspondingly, the contribution of micropores and mesopores was investigated in Fig. 3 f and Table S1. As expected, NGZC exhibits a higher pore volume (0.862 cm3/g) and specific surface area (1003 m2 /g) than those of ZC (0.858 cm3/g, 998 m2 /g). Particularly, the volumes of mesopores and micropores in NGZC were found to be 0.742 and 0.120 cm³ /g, respectively, representing 86.08 and 13.92 % of the total pore volume. In comparison, the mesopore and micropore volumes of ZC were found to be 0.401 and 0.457 cm3/g, respectively, which represent 46.74 and 53.26 % of the total pore volume. In other words, NGZC exhibits a higher specific surface area (1003 m²/g) compared to ZC (998 m²/g), despite having a significantly lower proportion of micropore volume (13.92 %) relative to ZC (53.26 %). This finding indicates that the reduced micropore content in NGZC does not compromise the overall specific surface area but instead enhances the accessibility and favorability of the surface for ion electrosorption. When compared to pristine ZC, the mesoporous-dominated structure of NGZC provides more accessible pore spaces for ion penetration, mitigating the limitations associated with micropores, such as ion exclusion effects caused by EDL overlapping. Moreover, the ratio of mesopore volume increased from 46.74 % for ZC to 86.08 % for NGZC, which demonstrates that the surfactant-mediated approach has been effective in enhancing the pore structure of the ZIF-8 derived carbon. The well-developed porosity of NGZC provide a substantial number of adsorption sites and transport channels for salt ions, thereby enhancing its electro-sorption capacity.

XPS was employed to investigate the surface chemical composition of NGZC and ZC, and the full spectrum plots and corresponding elemental contents are presented in Fig. 4a and Fig. 4b. Apparently, NGZC exhibits a higher N content (7.59 %) than ZC (6.89 %), suggesting the successful doping of nitrogen derived from N-GQDs on the surface of NGZC. The C1s ratio exceeds 85 % and is stable, indicating that both NGZC and ZC are representative carbon-based electrode materials. To gain further insight into the role of different N species in the desalination process, high-definition N 1 s spectra of NGZC and ZC are presented in Fig. 4c. The N 1 s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three peaks located at 397.8, 399.6 and 401.4 eV, which correspond to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N, respectively[13]. The ratios of pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N for NGZC are 35.27, 19.21 and 45.53 %, respectively (Fig. 4d). Notably, NGZC demonstrates higher quantity of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N than that of ZC, suggesting that the pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N play a more significant role than graphitic-N in the desalination process. This is because the pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N contribute electrons and produce defects, thereby creating channels and active sites for Na ions[36]. In contrast, the graphitic-N improves the electroconductivity of NGZC and ZC throughout the charge process and discharge processes [14].

As illustrated in Fig. 4e, the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum for NGZC reveals five distinct peaks, which can be attributed to C-C/CC (284.6 eV), C-O (285.8 eV), C-N (286.9 eV), CO (289.2), and C-CO (291.2 eV)[36]. Apparently, NGZC exhibits a higher proportion of C-N (10.8 %) than that of ZC (6.4 %), indicating that N-GQDs was successfully incorporated into NGZC composites. Furthermore, the prevalence of the C-C/CC bond suggests that both NGZC and ZC are highly carbonized ZIF-8-derived porous carbon materials.

Fig. 4 f demonstrates that the sp2 hybrid contribution in ZC and NGZC aligns with the findings regarding N species, thereby substantiating the successful doping of nitrogen into the carbon matrix of NGZC via covalent bonds[37]. Therefore, the NGZC, with three-dimensional hierarchical pore structure and appropriate N contents and species, provides abundant mass transfer channels and adsorption sites for sodium ions.

The electrochemical performance of NGZC and ZC was evaluated using a three-electrode system in a 1.0 mol/L NaCl solution. Fig. 5a presents the cyclic voltammetry (CV) results of NGZC and ZC at a sweep speed of 5 mV/s. Notably, the CV curves of NGZC and ZC exhibit a near-symmetric and quasi-rectangular shape devoid of redox peaks within the potential window of −1.0–1.0 V, suggesting a typical electronic double-layer capacitor (EDLC) behavior[38]. Moreover, NGZC displays a markedly larger integrated area than that of ZC, indicating a superior specific capacitance and enhanced electrochemical performance. This is further proved by the calculated specific capacitance of NGZC and ZC at varying scan rates, as illustrated in Fig. 5b. As the scan rate increases from 5 mV/s to 100 mV/s, NGZC exhibits a higher specific capacitance (185.43 F/g ∼ 283.16 F/g) than that of ZC. Particularly, at a scanning rate of 5 mV/s, NGZC exhibits the more favorable specific capacitance of 283.16 F/g, which is also higher than that of previously reported carbon electrodes [29,39]. The findings demonstrate that the introduction of N into the ZIF-8 pores significantly enhances the specific capacitance. This is due to the increased pyrrolic-N (19.21 at%) which enhances both the pseudo-capacitance and the electrical conductivity of NGZC, thereby promoting electrochemical active sites for ion adsorption [40]. In addition, the interpenetration of the three pore types, predominantly mesopores, gives rise to a three-dimensional network structure within NGZC, which facilitates ion transport and enhances the conductivity of NGZC [34]. Furthermore, the high degree of graphitization and the presence of significant defects in NGZC result in enhanced electrical conductivity and an improved charge transfer efficiency.

To better quantify the pseudocapacitive contribution in the CDI system, the authors conducted rigorous deconvolution of capacitive and diffusion-controlled contributions from CV data using Dunn’s method and Trasatti’s analysis[41,42]. First, we employed the Dunn’s method to analyze he relationship between current density (i) and scan rate (v) using the following equation:
(3)
i =
kv
b
Taking the logarithm of both sides yields:
(4)
log i = b log ( v ) + log ( k )
Here, the slope (b) represents the proportionality between the current and scan rate. A value of b = 1.0 suggests purely capacitive-controlled behavior, whereas b = 0.5 indicates diffusion-controlled behavior[43].

As presented in Fig. 5c, the calculated b-values for the ZC and NGZC electrodes are 0.86 and 0.92 (Fig. 5c), which are both close to 1.0, confirming their predominantly capacitive-controlled nature. This result aligns with the typical behavior of pseudocapacitive materials, where surface reactions dominate over bulk diffusion[42].

Second, we further applied Trasatti’s method to decomposes the total current into capacitive and diffusion-controlled components using the following equations:

corresponds to the diffusion-controlled contribution.

Figs. 5d and 5e reveal that the calculated capacitive contribution for NGZC increased significantly from 60.1 % at 5 mV/s to 89.0 % at 100 mV/s (Fig. 5d). In contrast, the capacitive contribution for ZC ranged from 50.5 % at 5 mV/s to 78.5 % at 100 mV/s (Fig. 5e). This enhancement in the capacitive contribution for NGZC highlights the significant role of pseudocapacitance in its superior charge storage capability. This is attributed to fact that the nitrogen doping introduces additional active sites on the graphene quantum dots, facilitating fast redox reactions and enhancing pseudocapacitive behavior, as illustrated in Fig. 4d.

Fig. 5 f illustrates the GCD curves of NGZC and ZC, which are triangular at a current density of 0.5 A/g, further proving that NGZC and ZC have good capacitive properties of the double layer[38]. Particularly, NGZC exhibits longer discharge times and smaller IR drops than that of ZC, indicating its excellent capacitive capability and minimal internal resistance[16]. To support this point, the specific capacitance of NGZC and ZC at different current densities was calculated in accordance with Eq. S2 and the findings are presented in Fig. 5 g. As expected, at a current density of 0.5 A/g, NGZC exhibits a higher specific capacitance (226.3 F/g) than that of ZC (118.2 F/g), which is in accordance with the CV experimental results (Fig. 5b). Even at an elevated current density of 5 A/g, the capacitance retention of NGZC remains at 81.7 %, which is significantly higher than that of ZC (49.9 %). This observation indicates that NGZC exhibits enhanced high-rate charging and discharging capabilities.

To further assess the ion-transport behavior and electrical resistance of NGZC and ZC, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed, and the resulting Nyquist plots are displayed in Fig. 5 h. The fitting equivalent circuit model ( Fig. 5i, Table S2) indicates that NGZC exhibits a lower charge transfer resistance (R ct) value of 1.11Ω than that of ZC (1.55Ω), reflecting a faster charge transfer rate at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Furthermore, in the low-frequency region, NGZC exhibited a higher slope of the EIS curve than that of ZC, indicating a rapid ion diffusion response and a superior ion adsorption fixation[38]. To sum up, NGZC demonstrated well-defined pore structure along with a larger mesoporous ratio and N-doping, which enhance the charge transfer capability and conductivity, resulting in excellent CDI performance[28].

The desalination performance of the freestanding NGZC electrodes was evaluated using a laboratory-built symmetric CDI device with a 250 mg/L NaCl solution, and a bias voltage of 1.2 V. As illustrated in Fig. 6a, once the voltage is imposed on the CDI cell, the solution conductivity decreases dramatically (orange region), signifying the rapid adsorption of ions onto the surface of the oppositely charged NGZC electrodes. In virtue of the electrostatic repulsion of the EDL, the electro-sorption equilibrium is almost reached after approximately 12 min of desalination, which agrees with the reported results[28]. Subsequently, when the voltage is removed, the solution conductivity increases rapidly back to the initial state (green part), indicating that the NGZC electrode exhibits facile adsorption and regeneration performance.

In addition, the electro-sorption capacity of NGZC, ZC and coal-based activated carbon (AC) was investigated using their respective conductivity curves, as illustrated in Fig. 6b. Initially, the ionic conductivity of the three materials decreases significantly within 10 min when applying 1.2 V voltage in a 100 mg/L NaCl solution, indicating a considerable adsorption of Na+/Cl- and a rapid formation of EDL [13]. Subsequently, the curves exhibit a gradual decline over time until reaching a state of complete saturation. Importantly, NGZC consistently exhibits the lowest conductivity at specific times, and reaches the equilibrium conductivity of 153.43 μS/cm, suggesting NGZC possesses the highest electro-sorption capacity.

The corresponding SAC values for NGZC, ZC and AC were calculated in accordance with the specifications outlined in Eq. S3, and illustrated in Fig. 6c. The dynamic SAC values of NGZC, ZC and AC increases gradually with prolonged running time (40 mins). Specifically, NGZC achieves a higher SAC of 10.49 mg/g than that of ZC (5.22 mg/g) and AC (3.23 mg/g), when applying a 100 mg/L NaCl solution, thereby demonstrating its exceptional capacity for capturing Na+ and Cl− ions. The higher SAC of NGZC can be attributed to the more favorable N doping. The presence of pyrrolic N and pyridine N can enhance the pseudo-capacitance by acting as electrochemically active sites, which facilitates an augmented adsorption capacity[40].

Moreover, a CDI Ragone plot was employed to visually evaluate the comprehensive desalination performance by taking both the SAC and SAR into consideration. Fig. 6d reveals that NGZC is situated in the upper right corner with higher SAC and SAR. Particularly, NGZC displays a more rapid SAR of 1.31 mg/g·min, indicating a superior desalination rate in comparison to ZC and AC. The enhanced desalination capacity of NGZC can be attributed to: (1) The high pore volume and rich mesoporous structure afford a convenient transmission channel and accelerate the rate of ion diffusion; (2) The high efficiently of N doping provides more accessible adsorption sites and improves the adsorption capacity via the rich pyridinic-N, and pyrrolic-N; (3) The high specific capacitance and low charge transfer resistance facilitate a the more favorable electrical double layer and the ion diffusion ability. Therefore, nitrogen doping effectively facilitates the charge transfer and ion transport of NGZC, thus improving its CDI performance.

The influence of working voltages on the CDI performance of NGZC was subsequently investigated to estimate its practical potential under 100 mg/L. The variation in conductivity of NGZC electrodes at different voltages versus CDI Ragone plots are shown in Figures S4-S5. As illustrated in Fig. 6e, the SAC value of NGZC exhibits a notable increase from 11.91 to 15.95 mg/g in conjunction with the elevation of voltage from 1.0 to 1.4 V. Due to the stronger electrostatic field resulting from the higher voltage, ion transport and EDL formation can be facilitated, thereby enhancing the electro-sorption capacity[44]. Interestingly, the enhancement of SAC values from 1.2 V to 1.4 V is less pronounced than that observed from 1.0 V to 1.2 V. This discrepancy can be attributed to the fact that the 1.4 V voltage exceeds the water electrolysis voltage of 1.23 V, resulting in limited electrolysis of water. It is possible that electrolysis may reduce the solvent of the salt solution, thereby offsetting some of the increase in the adsorption capacity caused by the voltage increase[45].

The effect of the initial NaCl concentration ranging from 100 mg/L to 500 mg/L on SAC is also evaluated (Fig. 6 f, Fig. S6, S7). Fig. 6 f reveals that NGZC exhibits enhanced SAC values with elevated initial concentrations, and the highest capacity of 17.86 mg/g is obtained with a NaCl concentration of 500 mg/L. This is due to the fact that higher saline concentrations facilitate the formation of electric double layers in a more expeditious manner, thereby reducing the resistance of the ion channels and consequently enhancing ion clearance[46]. Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. S6, S7, NGZC demonstrates a shorter CDI time (12 mins) for 500 mg/L NaCl than that for 250 mg/ L (21 mins) and 100 mg/L (40 mins) NaCl, resulting in an enhanced SAR of 2.06 mg /g∙ min at 500 mg/L. Therefore, increasing the working voltage and initial salt concentration would both enhance the SAC and SAR values of NGZC, suggesting excellent desalination performance of CDI. This is attributed to the GCS double electric layer theory model, which emphasizes that an increase in voltage and ion strength will result in a thinning of the diffuse layer in the electrode surface double electric layer, which is beneficial for charge transmission[47].

Furthermore, the long-term cycling stability of the CDI cell was evaluated in a 250 mg L−1 NaCl solution and an applied voltage of 1.2 V. The real-time fluctuations in conductivity during the circulation process are illustrated in Fig. 7a. As expected, NGZC exhibits remarkable cycling stability, retaining over 90 % of its SAC capacity after 30 cycles without any appreciable decline in performance (Fig. 7b). The results demonstrate the NGZC electrode's excellent cycle performance and reprocessing capability of NGZC electrode. In addition, these results suggest that the lower micropore content does not negatively impact the long-term CDI performance of this study. Instead, the higher proportion of mesopores enhances ion transport, increases the accessible surface area for electrosorption, and ensures sustained performance over extended periods. Notably, the superior CDI cycling stability together with high SAC value, which exceeds those of the reported N-doped carbons[48–52], as presented in Fig. 7d and Table S3. The notable cycling stability and desalination capacity of NGZC can be attributed to two primary factors. First, the surfactant-mediated approach has effectively enhanced the proportion of mesoporous, which serves as optimal active sites and transport channels for ion adsorption. This is corroborated by the findings presented in Fig. 3e and Fig. 3 f, which reveal that the ratio of mesopore volume has increased from 46.74 % for ZC to 86.08 % for NGZC. Second, the incorporation of N-GQDs has successfully increased the ratio of pyridinic-N, and pyrrolic-N, thereby enhancing pseudocapacitive activity and facilitate charge transfer for CDI desalination [53]. This is proved by the findings displayed in Fig. 4c and Fig. 4d, which demonstrate that NGZC possess the higher ratio of pyrrolic-N (19.21 %) than that of ZC (0 %).

Furthermore, the authors conducted a comprehensive analysis of the cost-effectiveness regarding the CDI desalination process, as summarized in Table S4. The total cost per milligram of Na⁺ capture (CNY/ mg Na+ capture) was calculated by considering three key factors: material cost, energy cost, and adsorption capacity. Notably, NGZC demonstrates the lowest total cost (0.45 CNY/ mg Na+ capture) among the four reported materials[48,49,52], showcasing its economic advantage. Moreover, the material cost of NGZC (0.24 CNY/mg) is significantly lower than that of OQAS-AC@NGO complexes (0.311 SGD/mg)[54], which were previously used for brackish water desalination. This cost reduction can be attributed to the adoption of a one-step synthesis method in our study, which not only simplifies the production process but also minimizes both material and energy expenses. Therefore, our lower production cost highlights the economic viability of NGZC for industrial-scale implementation, contributing to sustainable water treatment solutions.

Herein, our primary goal was not only to achieve high desalination and cycling performance but also to prevent secondary pollution caused by the leaching of toxic substances from the adsorbent. To this end, the authors conducted the quantity analysis of CTAB through spectrophotometric method reported by Hu et al.[55]. There is no CTAB was detected in the desalinated water, indicating that its concentration was below the detection limit (0.364 mg/L), which are both lower than the reported IC50 value (1.5 mg/L)[56]. This finding further confirms the absence of CTAB in the effluent, due to the two post-synthesis washing steps in the preparation protocol. Therefore, the environmental risk associated with CTAB in our study is minimal. In addition, extensive studies have demonstrated that N-GQDs possess minimal potential toxicity and environmental effects. Moreover, the low concentration (50 µg/mL) of N-GQDs used in our preparation process is 2 times lower than the reported IC50 value, further ensures their safety[57]. Therefore, these results confirm that the N-GQDs used in our synthesis pose negligible environmental and toxicological risks.

To gain deeper insight into the contribution of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N to desalination capacity, we normalized the combined contents of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N in atom percentage for reported N-doped electrodes[48–50] (Fig. 7c). In addition, we assessed the relationship between the combined contents (at%) of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N and the SACs of the reported N-doped electrodes, as displayed in Fig. 7d. In comparison to the previously reported N-doped electrodes, NGZC in this study exhibits the highest combined contents of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N (4.14 at%, Fig. 7c), accompanied by a more favorable SAC value (17.86 mg/g, Fig. 7d). Most interestingly, a strong linear correlation (R² = 0.935) was observed between the combined contents of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N and the SACs (Fig. 7d). This suggests that the combined content of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N is the key factor in determining the desalination performance, and that increasing the combined contents can effectively enhance the desalination capacity in terms of the reported N-doped electrodes. The dominant types of N species, pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N can improve the pseudo-capacitance by acting as electrochemically active sites, which are likely produced by the following reactions ( Fig. 8):
This finding will provide a theoretical basis for further improving the desalination performance of N-doped carbon electrodes in the practical CDI process.

The authors employed the DFT calculations to better understand the role of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N in enhancing the pseudocapacitance and SACs in CDI desalination process. Fig. 9 compare the adsorption energy and charge density differences between the nitrogen-doped configurations (graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N, and pyridinic-N), pristine graphene and Na⁺. Notably, Fig. 9 reveals negative adsorption energies of Na⁺ on the functional groups of NGZCs, indicating significant chemical interactions between Na⁺ and the nitrogen-doped configurations and pristine graphene.

Among the three nitrogen functional groups, the pyrrolic-N exhibits the highest adsorption energy for Na⁺ (-601.13 kJ/mol, Fig. 9 a), when comparing with pyridinic-N (-580.50 kJ/mol, Fig. 9b), graphitic-N (-429.38 kJ/mol, Fig. 9c) and graphene (-412.98 kJ/mol, Fig. 9d). This indicates that the presence of pyrrolic-N significantly improves the heteroatom active sites for Na⁺ capture, which is consistent with results illustrated in Fig. 7d. This is because, on the one hand, the increased amount of pyrrolic-N significantly enhances pseudocapacitive activity, thereby improving electrochemical active sites for ion adsorption[40,53]. On the other hand, the introduction of pyrrolic-N provides more defects in NGZC, thus enhancing the electrical conductivity and charge transfer efficiency.

In addition, based on the above results of adsorption energy, we further calculated the charge density differences between different N species (graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N, and pyridinic-N), pristine graphene and Na⁺. As presented in the bottom of Fig. 9a-d, the electron transfer between Na⁺ and pyrrolic-N/pyridinic-N is significantly stronger than that between Na⁺ and graphene/graphitic-N. In other words, pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N possess stronger electron depletion around Na⁺ compared to graphitic-N, indicating enhanced charge transfer. This improvement thereby enhances the surface reactivity of N-GQDs, enabling faster ion adsorption/desorption kinetics, which facilitate Na⁺ capture[53].

These results are consistent with the finding presented in Fig. 5b, Figs. 7c and 7d, where a strong linear correlation (R² = 0.935) was observed between the combined contents of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N and the SACs. The enhanced SAC can be attributed to the increased pyrrolic-N content (19.21 at%) in NGZC, which not only improves pseudo-capacitance but also enhances electrical conductivity. These improvements collectively promote the formation of electrochemically active sites for efficient ion adsorption, thus contributing significantly to the overall desalination performance[40].

In summary, the lower adsorption energy and higher charge transfer at pyridinic/pyrrolic-N sites confirm their role as redox-active centers for pseudocapacitance. In other word, the DFT calculations provide additional theoretical support to validate the roles of pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N in enhancing pseudocapacitance. In addition, the DFT calculations provide strong evidence that pyrrolic-N plays a significantly role in enhancing Na⁺ adsorption, as demonstrated by its significantly higher adsorption energy compared to other nitrogen species and pristine graphene.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized a novel N-doped carbon material NGZC via a one-step in-situ growth strategy, thereby creating a highly effective electrode for CDI. Due to the introduction of surfactant and N-doped graphene quantum dots, the NGZC obtained a more favorable mesopore volume (0.742 cm3/g) and pyrrolic-N ratio (19.21 %), resulting in its fast ion transport and excellent pseudocapacitive activity. This achievement leverages a higher specific capacitance (283.16 F/g) than that of the pristine ZC (144.4 F/g) when tested at a sweep speed of 5 mV/s. As a result, the optimized NGZC achieves an excellent SAC (17.86 mg/g) and SAR (2.06 mg/g∙ min) when 500 mg/L NaCl is applied at 1.2 V, surpassing most of those reported electrode materials. In addition, the NGZC electrode exhibited a brilliant 90 % SAC retention after the cyclic regeneration test, demonstrating excellent practical potential for seawater desalination. Furthermore, DFT was also conducted to elaborate the disparity of sodium adsorption energy among the nitrogen species for in-depth understanding, and it mainly benefits from the ascendency of the pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N over the graphitic-N dopant. This synergistic combination of precise control of hierarchical porosity and appropriate regulation of pyrrolic-N holds great promise in paving the way for novel approaches to enhance the electrochemical performance of CDI.
